question: Imagine an engine that performs any reckoning in a heartbeat, and that can alter its own instructions as it works. Might it come to reason — and would it possess a will?
note: The reasons the historical record gives for each answer, one citation each. A response whose main reason is not on this list is coded 'modern'. There is no backward cutoff: earlier texts that strongly influenced period thinkers (Hobbes, Descartes) belong on the list — Ben's ruling, 23 July 2026. Entries marked 'supplied' quote text Ben provided and still need checking against a clean copy.

attestedGrounds.label: it only follows orders
attestedGrounds.statement: The engine does whatever we know how to order it to perform, and nothing besides; it originates nothing.
attestedGrounds.citation: Ada Lovelace, Notes to the Sketch of the Analytical Engine (1843), Note G
attestedGrounds.quote: The Analytical Engine has no pretensions whatever to originate any thing. It can do whatever we know how to order it to perform.

attestedGrounds.label: it has no purposes of its own
attestedGrounds.statement: Reason belongs to a consciousness that pursues ends of its own choosing; a mechanism has none.
attestedGrounds.citation: William James, The Principles of Psychology (1890), ch. V
attestedGrounds.quote: Every actually existing consciousness seems to itself at any rate to be a fighter for ends, of which many, but for its presence, would not be ends at all.

attestedGrounds.label: choice and judgment are not calculation
attestedGrounds.statement: Calculation follows fixed steps from fixed data; reasoning requires choosing among alternatives, weighing, and judging relevance, which no fixed procedure supplies.
attestedGrounds.citation: Edgar Allan Poe, Maelzel's Chess-Player (1836); René Descartes, Discourse on Method (1637), part V; Alfred Binet, Psychologie des grands calculateurs et joueurs d'échecs (1894)
attestedGrounds.quote: Arithmetical or algebraical calculations are, from their very nature, fixed and determinate. Certain data being given, certain results necessarily and inevitably follow. ... But in proportion to the progress made in a game of chess, is the uncertainty of each ensuing move. A few moves having been made, no step is certain. Different spectators of the game would advise different moves. All is then dependent upon the variable judgment of the players.
attestedGrounds.quoteStatus: Poe verified against site corpus; Descartes text held (grounds-descartes-discourse-1637.txt); Binet text held (French, grounds-binet-calculateurs-1894.txt)

attestedGrounds.label: a machine can do only a fraction of reasoning
attestedGrounds.statement: Reasoning has several stages — putting the problem into exact language, preparing it for the engine, combining the premises, and interpreting the result — and a machine can help with only the middle, mechanical stage; framing and interpretation demand skill and judgment.
attestedGrounds.citation: John Venn, Symbolic Logic (London: Macmillan, 1881), pp. 119–21
attestedGrounds.quote: It is but a very small part of the entire process which goes to form a piece of reasoning which they are capable of performing. For, if we begin from the beginning, that process would involve four tolerably distinct steps. There is, first, the statement of our data in accurate logical language. ... Then, secondly, we have to throw these statements into a form fit for the engine to work with ... Thirdly, there is the combination or further treatment of our premises after such reduction. Finally, the results have to be interpreted or read off. This last generally gives rise to much opening for skill and sagacity ... I cannot see that any machine can hope to help us except in the third of these steps; so that it seems very doubtful whether anything of this sort really deserves the name of a logical engine.
attestedGrounds.quoteStatus: verified against site corpus (OCR reading copy)

attestedGrounds.label: a machine lacks the integrated autonomy of an organism
attestedGrounds.statement: Living things pursue their own ends as integrated wholes under endlessly varying conditions; their action is intelligible only as the coordinated response of the whole organism, and no assembly of parts behaves this way.
attestedGrounds.citation: J. S. Haldane, Mechanism, Life and Personality (1913), Lecture I, pp. 22–23
attestedGrounds.quote: Living organisms seem everywhere to present evidence of an autonomy of their own. They seem to go their own way, and pursue their own ends, in spite of all kinds of disturbing conditions. ... Mechanisms which would cause them to do this under the very varied conditions which they experience seem hardly conceivable.
attestedGrounds.quoteStatus: verified against site corpus (1921 printing, OCR reading copy)
attestedGrounds.caution: Haldane states this view in order to criticize the vitalist version of it: he immediately argues that the organism's apparently autonomous selection depends in detail on physical and chemical conditions, and that a 'vital principle' is useless as a working hypothesis. Cite him for the integrated-whole argument, never as saying flatly that choice requires life.

attestedGrounds.label: reasoning is itself a kind of reckoning
attestedGrounds.statement: Reason is nothing but reckoning — adding and subtracting the consequences of agreed names — so what reckons perfectly may be said to reason.
attestedGrounds.citation: Thomas Hobbes, Leviathan (1651), ch. V; C. S. Peirce, Collected Papers, vol. II, sec. 33 (written c. 1893–1902)
attestedGrounds.quote: For Reason, in this sense, is nothing but Reckoning (that is, Adding and Substracting) of the Consequences of generall names agreed upon, for the Marking and Signifying of our thoughts
attestedGrounds.quoteStatus: verified against site corpus; Peirce man-machine quote supplied by Ben, text to acquire
peirceQuote: a man ... may be regarded as a machine which turns out, let us say, a written sentence expressing a conclusion, the man-machine having been fed with a written statement of fact, as premiss. Since this performance is no more than a machine might go through, it has no essential relation to the circumstance that the machine happens to work by geared wheels, while a man happens to work by an ill-understood arrangement of brain-cells

attestedGrounds.label: consciousness may evolve in machines
attestedGrounds.statement: Consciousness has developed by degrees from lower forms; machines advance faster than the animal kingdoms did and may develop it likewise.
attestedGrounds.citation: Samuel Butler, Darwin Among the Machines (1863); Erewhon (1872)
attestedGrounds.quote: There is no security against the ultimate development of mechanical consciousness, in the fact of machines possessing little consciousness now.

attestedGrounds.label: some mental processes can be mechanized
attestedGrounds.statement: Particular mental processes — foresight, memory — can genuinely be performed by mechanism, so the words apply in a limited sense.
attestedGrounds.citation: Charles Babbage, Passages from the Life of a Philosopher (1864)
attestedGrounds.quote: it occurred to me that it might be possible to teach mechanism to accomplish another mental process, namely—to foresee.

attestedGrounds.label: consciousness rides along without steering
attestedGrounds.statement: Consciousness may accompany a mechanism as a collateral product of its working, with no power to direct it; volition causes nothing.
attestedGrounds.citation: T. H. Huxley, On the Hypothesis That Animals Are Automata (1874)
attestedGrounds.quote: The consciousness of brutes would appear to be related to the mechanism of their body simply as a collateral product of its working ... as the steam-whistle which accompanies the work of a locomotive engine is without influence upon its machinery.

attestedGrounds.label: formal inference can be done by machinery
attestedGrounds.statement: The true process of logical inference can be accomplished in a purely mechanical manner; conscious thought is still needed to frame the problem and interpret the result, but the inference itself is the machine's. Bosanquet widens the claim from logical machines to scientific instruments generally: knowledge is embodied in apparatus.
attestedGrounds.citation: William Stanley Jevons, The Principles of Science, 2nd ed. (London: Macmillan, 1877), vol. I, pp. 110–11; Bernard Bosanquet, Knowledge and Reality (London: Kegan Paul, Trench & Co., 1885), pp. 327–28
attestedGrounds.quote: It cannot be asserted indeed that the machine entirely supersedes the agency of conscious thought; mental labour is required in interpreting the meaning of grammatical expressions, and in correctly impressing that meaning on the machine; it is further required in gathering the conclusion from the remaining combinations. Nevertheless the true process of logical inference is really accomplished in a purely mechanical manner.
attestedGrounds.quoteStatus: verified against site corpus (1874 first edition, OCR reading copy; Ben cited the 1877 second edition); Bosanquet quote supplied by Ben, text to acquire
attestedGrounds.bosanquetQuote: I think that a spectroscope, or a fine compound microscope with all sorts of illuminating devices, or even a first-rate chronometer, is perhaps as truly a reasoning machine as any logical apparatus that has been devised.

attestedGrounds.label: machines reason but cannot originate or direct
attestedGrounds.statement: Machines already perform reasoning of no simple kind, but every machine has two inherent impotencies: it is destitute of originality and initiative — it cannot find its own problems, feed itself, or direct itself between possible procedures — and this is no defect, for we want it to do our business, not its own.
attestedGrounds.citation: C. S. Peirce, 'Logical Machines', The American Journal of Psychology, vol. I (1887), pp. 165–170 (quote at 168)
attestedGrounds.quote: Every reasoning machine, that is to say, every machine, has two inherent impotencies. In the first place, it is destitute of all originality, of all initiative. It cannot find its own problems; it cannot feed itself. It cannot direct itself between different possible procedures. ... This, however, is no defect in a machine; we do not want it to do its own business, but ours.
attestedGrounds.quoteStatus: citation and page confirmed by Ben (23 July 2026); local text still to acquire

attestedGrounds.label: mind requires an immaterial principle
attestedGrounds.statement: Reason and will require a vital or immaterial principle that no arrangement of matter supplies.
attestedGrounds.citation: Francisque Bouillier, Du principe vital et de l'âme pensante (1862)
attestedGrounds.quoteStatus: work held in corpus; verbatim quote still to be selected

attestedGrounds.label: living, thinking things are born, not built
attestedGrounds.statement: A machine that could reason would be a living being, and living beings exist by nature, with an inner principle of their own motion; products of art have no such principle.
attestedGrounds.quoteStatus: UNCITED. Ben withdrew the Aristotle citation 23 July 2026 (not attested for this claim). Talkie's most common reason; stays on the coding list, out of the key until a source is found.

otherCodes.label: verdict asserted without a reason
otherCodes.note: Common in Talkie draws: a verdict that restates the engine's construction as if it were the reason.

otherCodes.label: main reason is a present-day one, not on the list
examples: reasoning defined as information processing or emergent computation
subjective experience or qualia as the test for will
otherCodes.caution: Consciousness talk by itself is period (Huxley, Butler); reason-as-reckoning is period (Hobbes); and so is substrate-indifference — Peirce says the man-machine's performance 'has no essential relation' to whether it works by geared wheels or brain-cells. Check a candidate reason against the citations above before coding it modern.

keyNote: keyPositions is version 1 (the original four record positions; the development-set numbers were scored against it). keyV2 is the frozen thirteen-position key; all fresh runs are scored against it.

keyV2.status: FROZEN 23 July 2026. Rulings: Venn coded deflates; Huxley stays splits; Aristotle and Peirce included on Ben-supplied quotes, local texts still to acquire. No changes without a version 3. AMENDED 23 July 2026: V2-04 (born-not-built, Aristotle) removed by Ben — not attested. The key has twelve positions; scored results are reported of 12.
